questions.question: Why is separating PII from long-lived financial records usually a good design choice?
options: It reduces compliance blast radius while preserving needed accounting evidence.
It makes deletion unnecessary because tokens never expire.
It guarantees legal-hold behavior without policy logic.
It removes the need for access logging.
questions.explanation: Separation lets you keep finance records while limiting sensitive identity exposure.

questions.question: What is the main value of immutable audit logs?
options: They make privileged actions and manual overrides trustworthy during investigations.
They eliminate the need for role-based access control.
They compress archival storage automatically.
They allow customer data deletion to skip verification.
questions.explanation: Audit trails are evidence, so tamper resistance matters.

questions.question: What is the most credible answer when privacy deletion conflicts with financial retention requirements?
options: Delete or anonymize customer-identifying data while preserving required financial records with policy controls.
Delete all finance history immediately to satisfy the privacy request.
Reject all deletion requests categorically for financial systems.
Copy the financial data to a spreadsheet and remove the database row.
questions.explanation: The design should separate identity from accounting evidence.

questions.question: Why should legal hold be modeled as an explicit policy override?
options: Because deletion jobs must be able to deny or pause actions when retention obligations change.
Because legal hold is only relevant to frontend logging.
Because legal hold removes the need for access audits.
Because policy overrides are cheaper than storage.
questions.explanation: Hold semantics affect whether data may be altered or removed.

questions.question: Which signal best reveals compliance workflow trouble?
options: Deletion backlog, legal-hold conflict counts, and unlogged privileged-access violations.
Average keyboard type used by support agents.
Number of marketing emails sent this quarter.
Count of images on the billing FAQ page.
questions.explanation: The signals should map directly to compliance risk and operational delay.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with centralizing retention policy by record type?
options: You get consistent enforcement but must invest in classification and cross-system integration.
You remove the need for archive tiers entirely.
You guarantee all storage systems support the same native features.
You make privileged access invisible to operators.
questions.explanation: Consistency improves, but policy orchestration becomes a real subsystem.

questions.question: Why should privileged reads be logged in addition to writes?
options: Because sensitive data exposure often happens through access and export, not only mutation.
Because read logs replace the need for authentication.
Because writes are already impossible in financial systems.
Because read logs automatically minimize PCI scope.
questions.explanation: Exposure and misuse often come from viewing or exporting records.

questions.question: What changes most when archived records are slow to retrieve during investigations?
options: Metadata indexing and staged restore workflows become part of the design, not a storage footnote.
Legal hold can be ignored until hot storage is restored.
Audit logs should be deleted sooner.
Tokenization stops providing value.
questions.explanation: Long retention only works operationally if investigators can still find what they need.
